title: Family Change: Divorce, Cohabitation and New Household Types
examTip: When explaining family change, always give both a cause and a sociological perspective — not just the statistic. 'Divorce rose because of secularisation (Functionalists would see this as social fragmentation)' is worth more marks.
introTips: Know the key dates: Divorce Reform Act 1969, Matrimonial and Family Proceedings Act 1984, no-fault divorce 2022.
Be able to explain at least four reasons why divorce has increased — legal, economic, cultural, social.
The 'beanpole family' (Brannen, 1999) is a favourite concept — long, thin network across generations.
intro: Why are families in the UK so different from 50 years ago? In 1970, most people married young, stayed married, and had children within marriage. Today, nearly half of marriages end in divorce, 3.6 million couples cohabit without marrying, and 8 million people live alone. These aren't random changes — they reflect deep shifts in law, economics, gender roles, and what people expect from relationships. Sociologists disagree sharply about whether these changes are progress, loss, or simply diversity.

keyPoints.heading: Divorce trends and explanations
keyPoints.content: The Divorce Reform Act 1969 was transformative: for the first time, spouses could divorce on grounds of 'irretrievable breakdown' without proving fault or blame. Divorce rates spiked. The Matrimonial and Family Proceedings Act 1984 reduced the minimum waiting time to one year, raising rates further. The peak was around 2003 (~150,000 divorces/year in England and Wales); rates have since declined to ~100,000/year partly because fewer people marry in the first place. Around 42% of marriages end in divorce. Women petition for divorce more often than men (approximately 60% of petitions). No-fault divorce was introduced in England and Wales in April 2022. Explanations for rising divorce: (1) Legal changes — cheaper and easier (Legal Aid); (2) Secularisation — less religious stigma; (3) Women's financial independence — employed women and the welfare state mean women can afford to leave unhappy marriages; (4) Rising expectations — Giddens' (1992) 'pure relationship' should provide emotional fulfilment; when it doesn't, people leave; (5) Longer lives — more time for partners to grow apart.
writeBullets: Divorce Reform Act 1969 — irretrievable breakdown sufficient; no need to prove fault
1984 Act — reduced waiting time to 1 year; rates rose further
Peak ~150,000 divorces/year (2003); now ~100,000/year; 42% of marriages end in divorce
Women petition more (~60%); no-fault divorce introduced 2022
Explanations: legal changes, secularisation, women's financial independence, rising expectations, longer lives
Giddens (1992): 'pure relationship' — must provide emotional fulfilment or partners leave
keyPoints.memTip: SLEEP = Secularisation, Legal changes, Expectations (rising), Economics (women's independence), People living longer — five divorce causes.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often cite only legal changes as a reason for rising divorce — remind them to give multiple causes.

keyPoints.heading: Cohabitation and changing marriage patterns
keyPoints.content: Cohabitation (unmarried couples living together) has become the norm before marriage in the UK. There are now 3.6 million cohabiting couples — the fastest-growing family type. Marriage rates are at a historic low: approximately 225,000 marriages in 2019, down from 400,000 in 1970. The average age of first marriage has risen sharply — women now marry at 31, men at 33 on average (2022). Reasons for declining marriage: secularisation (religion less central to life decisions), changing gender norms (women no longer financially dependent on marriage), financial pressures (house prices, cost of weddings), and the idea of 'trial marriage' (testing the relationship first). Postmodernists (Giddens, Beck) celebrate cohabitation as individual freedom and conscious choice. The New Right (Murray, Morgan) are concerned: cohabiting relationships are statistically less stable than marriages and provide weaker legal protection for partners (especially women) on separation. Note: cohabiting couples have far fewer legal rights than married couples — they cannot inherit automatically, have no automatic pension rights, and have limited financial claims on separation.
writeBullets: 3.6m cohabiting couples in UK — fastest-growing family type
Marriages: ~400,000/year (1970) → ~225,000 (2019) — historic low
Average age of first marriage: women 31, men 33 (2022)
Reasons: secularisation, gender equality, financial pressures, 'trial marriage' idea
New Right: cohabitation is less stable; fewer legal rights than marriage
Postmodernists: cohabitation reflects individual freedom and choice
keyPoints.memTip: Marriage is declining, cohabitation is rising — both trends have the same root causes: secularisation and women's independence.
keyPoints.tutor_note: AQA may ask students to evaluate cohabitation — always contrast New Right (negative) with Postmodern (positive) views.

keyPoints.heading: Changing birth rates and family size
keyPoints.content: The UK birth rate (live births per 1,000 population) has been falling for decades. The fertility rate was 1.56 children per woman in 2022 — well below the replacement level of 2.1. The average family now has 1.8 children. Reasons for declining birth rates: women prioritising careers (second-wave feminism opened educational and professional opportunities); the high cost of raising children (estimated £160,000 to age 18 in the UK); improved access to contraception; secularisation (fewer religious objections to contraception or childlessness); housing costs. Immigration has partly maintained overall population growth. The key consequence is an ageing population — more elderly people relative to working-age people, with major implications for the NHS, pension system, and social care. Some LICs still have high birth rates — the demographic transition model explains how countries move from high birth/death rates to low birth/death rates as they develop economically.
writeBullets: UK fertility rate 1.56 (2022) — below replacement level of 2.1
Average family: 1.8 children; household size declining
Reasons: women's careers, cost of children (~£160,000 to 18), contraception, secularisation, housing costs
Ageing population: more elderly, fewer workers — pressure on NHS, pensions, care
Immigration has partly offset falling birth rate
Demographic transition model: countries move from high to low birth rates as they develop
keyPoints.memTip: CASH = Careers (women's), Ageing population (consequence), Secularisation, Housing costs — key reasons for falling birth rate.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Link falling birth rates to ageing population consequences — this shows broader sociological thinking.

keyPoints.heading: Alternative household types
keyPoints.content: Modern Britain contains many household arrangements that don't fit traditional family categories. Living alone: around 8 million people in the UK live alone, making up 30% of all households — including young adults, divorced people, and elderly widows/widowers. LAT (Living Apart Together): couples in committed romantic relationships who choose to live in separate homes — a growing category especially among older adults. Chosen families (Weeks et al, 1999): particularly important in LGBTQ+ communities where close friends provide the emotional support and practical help that biological families often provide — or don't provide when biological family is unsupportive. The beanpole family (Brannen, 1999): as families have fewer children but live longer, the family structure becomes long and thin — multiple generations alive simultaneously (great-grandparents, grandparents, parents, children) but with very few siblings at each level. Contrasts with the broader, multi-sibling family networks of the past.
writeBullets: Living alone: 8m people in UK (30% of households) — young adults, divorced, elderly
LAT (Living Apart Together): committed couples in separate homes — growing especially among older adults
Chosen families (Weeks et al, 1999): close friends as family — especially in LGBTQ+ communities
Beanpole family (Brannen, 1999): long, thin — multiple generations alive but few siblings
Contrast beanpole with past 'broad' multi-sibling extended families
keyPoints.memTip: Beanpole = tall and thin: lots of generations (tall) but few siblings at each level (thin). Draw it!
keyPoints.tutor_note: The beanpole family is a high-value concept — many students don't know it but it's distinctive and memorable.

keyPoints.heading: Sociological perspectives on family change
keyPoints.content: Sociologists disagree profoundly about whether family change is positive or negative. The march of progress view (Fletcher, 1966) argues the family is becoming better: more loving, more private, more equal, more democratic. Divorce reflects high expectations — people won't settle for unhappy marriages. Feminist view (Oakley, Sharpe) — family change reflects women's liberation and growing financial independence; divorce and cohabitation give women more choices; but structural inequalities (pay gap, domestic labour) persist. The New Right (Murray, Morgan) — family breakdown causes social problems: higher crime rates, welfare dependency, educational underperformance among children from lone-parent families; traditional married families should be promoted through government policy. Postmodern view (Beck and Beck-Gernsheim, 1995; Stacey) — change reflects the 'individualisation' of modern society; people now construct their own life narratives including family arrangements; this is liberating but creates anxiety and instability.
writeBullets: March of progress (Fletcher, 1966): family improving — more equal, loving, voluntary
Feminist view: change reflects women's liberation; more choices; structural inequality persists
New Right (Murray): family breakdown → crime, welfare dependency; promote traditional marriage
Postmodern view (Beck and Beck-Gernsheim, 1995): individualisation — people construct own family choices
Stacey (1990): no single 'the family'; just 'families'; diversity is the postmodern reality
keyPoints.memTip: Four views on change: Progress → Feminist → New Right → Postmodern (PFNP). Each has a different emotional tone: optimism, liberation, alarm, freedom.
keyPoints.tutor_note: For extended writing, the strongest answers contrast at least two perspectives on whether change is positive or negative.

tables.title: Factors in family change: causes and sociological perspectives
rows: 1969 Act; secularisation; women's independence; rising expectations (Giddens)
New Right: social problem. Feminists: women's liberation. Postmodern: individual freedom
Secularisation; gender equality; financial pressures; cohabitation norm
Postmodern: free choice. New Right: weakens social stability
Secularisation; trial marriage; women's independence; financial pressures
Postmodern: positive. New Right: less stable, fewer legal rights
Women's careers; cost; contraception; secularisation; housing
Key consequence: ageing population — pressure on NHS and pensions
Divorce; later marriage; ageing population; LAT relationships
Postmodern: individualisation. New Right: social isolation
Brannen (1999): structural shift in family networks

0.question: Which piece of legislation in 1969 dramatically increased the divorce rate in England and Wales?
0.correctFeedback: Correct! The Divorce Reform Act 1969 allowed divorce on grounds of irretrievable breakdown — no need to prove fault — which triggered a sharp rise in the divorce rate.
0.wrongFeedback: The key legislation is the Divorce Reform Act 1969, which introduced 'irretrievable breakdown' as sufficient grounds for divorce, removing the need to prove fault.

3.question: What does the sociological term 'beanpole family' (Brannen, 1999) describe?
options: A long, thin family network with multiple generations alive but very few siblings at each level
A family where the mother is the main breadwinner
A family formed after divorce and remarriage with children from previous relationships
A cohabiting family that has not married
3.correctFeedback: Exactly right. The beanpole family (Brannen, 1999) is long and thin — multiple generations alive simultaneously (great-grandparents through to grandchildren) but very few siblings at each level.
3.wrongFeedback: The beanpole family (Brannen, 1999) describes a family structure that is long and thin — multiple generations alive because people live longer, but very few siblings because families have fewer children.

commonMistakes: Saying divorce rates are 'at an all-time high' — they actually peaked around 2003 and have since declined, partly because fewer people marry.
Listing only legal changes to explain rising divorce — you need at least three factors (legal, economic, cultural) for a full answer.
Confusing cohabitation statistics with marriage statistics — always specify which you're referring to.
Forgetting the 'beanpole family' concept (Brannen, 1999) — it's distinctive and not always covered but highly valued in exams.
Presenting family change as purely negative (New Right) without acknowledging feminist or postmodern perspectives that see it positively.
examTips: For a 'describe one change in family life' question: name the trend + give a statistic + explain one cause. Three elements for full marks.

When evaluating perspectives on family change, contrast at least two: 'While Murray (New Right) sees rising divorce as social breakdown, Giddens (1992) argues it reflects higher expectations of the pure relationship…'
Always anchor statistics to a date: '42% of marriages end in divorce' not just 'divorce is common'.
The 'march of progress' view (Fletcher, 1966) is useful as a starting point to then evaluate critically — examiners like seeing you challenge it.
revisionCardBullets: Divorce Reform Act 1969 — irretrievable breakdown sufficient; triggered steep rise in divorce
42% of marriages end in divorce; ~100,000/year; women petition more (~60%)
No-fault divorce introduced England and Wales 2022
Giddens (1992): 'pure relationship' — maintained only while emotionally fulfilling
3.6m cohabiting couples in UK — fastest-growing family type
Marriages: 400,000/year (1970) → 225,000 (2019); average first marriage age: women 31, men 33
Fertility rate 1.56 (2022) — below replacement level 2.1; ageing population consequence
Beanpole family (Brannen, 1999): long, thin — many generations, few siblings
Chosen families (Weeks, 1999): close friends as family, especially LGBTQ+ communities
8m people live alone in UK (30% of households)
New Right (Murray): family change → social problems; promote traditional marriage
Postmodern view (Beck and Beck-Gernsheim, 1995): individualisation — people construct own family choices
